food_name: Nam Yue (Cantonese Red Fermented Bean Curd)

local_names.tamil: சிவப்பு புளித்த பீன் தயிர் (Sivappu pulitha bean tayir)
local_names.hindi: लाल किण्वित बीन दही (Lal kinvit bean dahi)

region_of_origin: Guangdong, South China; widely used in Singapore Cantonese cooking
food_subgroup: Fermented bean curd condiment — red yeast rice (Monascus purpureus) fermented tofu

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for nam yue / red fermented bean curd specifically. Closest parent concept: fermented tofu condiment (FOODON:03301710 — tofu, fermented). No exact match available as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fermented soybean curd (tofu) — closest parent used for Cantonese red fermented bean curd with red yeast rice (nam yue); exact FoodEx2 code for nam yue not available
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0TFB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for fermented tofu products. No specific code exists for red yeast rice-fermented tofu (nam yue) in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for nam yue or red fermented bean curd as a food product.

preparation_method: aged in rice wine and salt brine
main_ingredients: red yeast rice (Monascus purpureus fermented rice)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of nam yue is 1 tablespoon (approximately 15g), typically used as a cooking marinade ingredient for Cantonese char siu or braised pork, or occasionally as a direct condiment on plain rice.

nutrient_source.title: China Food Composition Table (6th edition, Yang Yuexin ed., 2019) — Red Fermented Bean Curd (红腐乳/南乳); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fermented tofu (FDC ID 172444)
nutrient_source.organization: Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention / Peking University Health Science Center (China FCT 2019); USDA Agricultural Research Service (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from China Food Composition Table 2019 (Yang Yuexin ed.) for red fermented bean curd (南乳/红腐乳), which is the most authoritative regional source for this product. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fermented tofu entry for macro plausibility. China FCT 2019 is an approved regional primary source equivalent to FAO/INFOODS standards. Values are consistent between both sources within expected variation for fermented tofu products.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI from analogous fermented soy product — closest reference: fermented soybean products (GI ~20–25) per University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on fermented tofu
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nam yue (red fermented bean curd) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~20 based on analogous fermented soy products (fermented tofu, miso, tempeh) which consistently show GI 15–25 due to high protein and fat content, low digestible starch, and fermentation-mediated reduction of glycaemic response. GL per 15g serving = (20 × 1.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.32, rounded to 0.3 — classified as negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content: 3,091mg sodium per 100g (464mg per 15g tablespoon serving)
hypertension.guidance: Nam yue is very high in sodium (3,091mg/100g); hypertensive patients should restrict use to a cooking marinade ingredient shared across 4–6 portions (contributing ~75–115mg sodium per person) and must avoid consuming it directly as a condiment, where 1–2 tablespoons would deliver 464–928mg sodium per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic index (~20) and negligible glycaemic load per serving (GL ~0.3)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Nam yue has a negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~20, GL ~0.3 per 15g tablespoon) due to its predominantly protein-fat composition and fermentation-reduced starch; it is safe for people with Type 2 Diabetes when used as a cooking condiment in standard quantities, though its high sodium content requires monitoring in patients with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content is the primary cardiovascular concern; low saturated fat (1.2g/100g) and zero cholesterol are favourable; Monascus monacolins present at sub-therapeutic trace levels
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the primary concern with nam yue is its very high sodium content (3,091mg/100g), which can elevate blood pressure; saturated fat is low (1.2g/100g) and cholesterol is absent, which are favourable, but use should be restricted to small cooking quantities and not as a direct condiment to limit sodium exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones (genistein aglycone from fermentation), iron (3.5mg/100g), calcium (112mg/100g), and Monascus antioxidant pigments
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nam yue provides modest anti-inflammatory soy isoflavones in aglycone form (enhanced bioavailability from fermentation), along with iron and calcium relevant to RA nutritional needs; high sodium is the limiting factor if used as a direct condiment rather than a cooking ingredient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nam Yue (南乳) is a Cantonese red fermented bean curd, a traditional Chinese condiment with over 2,000 years of production history originating in Guangdong, South China. It is made by fermenting firm tofu cubes in a brine of Shaoxing rice wine, salt, spices, and red yeast rice (hong qu mi, produced by Monascus purpureus mould), which imparts the characteristic deep red-orange colour, umami-rich flavour, and distinctive fermented aroma. The texture is soft, creamy, and paste-like, with an intensely savoury, slightly sweet, and mildly alcoholic flavour profile. Nam yue is widely sold in Singapore at NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, and Sheng Siong under the Amoy and Lee Kum Kee brands. It is an essential ingredient in Cantonese char siu (BBQ pork) marinades, braised pork belly, and various Cantonese stir-fry sauces. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 153 kcal, 10.6g protein, 8.1g fat, 10.7g carbohydrate, and 3,091mg sodium — making it extremely high in sodium and suitable only as a cooking ingredient used in small quantities. Per standard 15g tablespoon serving: ~23 kcal, 1.6g protein, 1.2g fat, 1.6g carbohydrate, 464mg sodium. GI is approximately 20 (low), GL per serving ~0.3 (negligible). Contains Monascus monacolins at trace sub-therapeutic levels. Vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free; NOT halal due to Shaoxing rice wine content. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium — hypertensive and cardiovascular patients should use it only as a shared cooking ingredient, not as a direct condiment.
meal_context: Singapore hawker centre Cantonese cooking ingredient

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from China Food Composition Table 2019 (Yang Yuexin ed.) — the authoritative regional composition database for Chinese food products — cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fermented tofu entry. Values are internally consistent and plausible for a high-sodium fermented soy condiment. GI is an analogous estimate from approved University of Sydney GI Database fermented soy product references, clearly documented as estimated.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for red fermented bean curd (nam yue) not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or China FCT 2019 at the level of individual fatty acid classes; total fat and saturated fat are reported. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source consulted. 2. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per China FCT 2019 and USDA SR Legacy fermented tofu; this is a plant-based fermented product with no industrial trans fat. cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero; nam yue is a plant-based soy product. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per China FCT 2019 for red fermented bean curd. 3. GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate based on fermented soy product class (fermented tofu, miso) from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for nam yue exists in the published literature as of 2024. 4. Halal status: NOT halal — contains Shaoxing rice wine (alcohol). 5. Monascus monacolins present at trace levels in 1 tablespoon (15g) — sub-therapeutic dose for cholesterol lowering; clinically irrelevant at standard condiment use quantities. 6. FoodOn URI not available for nam yue specifically; closest parent concept used. FoodEx2 code A0TFB used as closest parent for fermented soy/tofu products.
